elements.sent-2411-007: Recently, Text-to-Image (T2I) synthesis technology has made tremendous strides.
elements.sent-2411-008: Numerous representative T2I models have emerged and achieved promising application outcomes, such as DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, Imagen, etc. In practice, it has become increasingly popular for model developers to selectively adopt various pre-trained text encoders and conditional diffusion models from third-party platforms, integrating them to build customized (personalized) T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, such an adoption approach is vulnerable to backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this work, we propose a Combinational Backdoor Attack against Customized T2I models (CBACT2I) targeting this application scenario.
elements.sent-2411-011: Different from previous backdoor attacks against T2I models, CBACT2I embeds the backdoor into the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model separately.
elements.sent-2411-012: The customized T2I model exhibits backdoor behaviors only when the backdoor text encoder is used in combination with the backdoor conditional diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-013: These properties make CBACT2I more stealthy and flexible than prior backdoor attacks against T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-014: Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of CBACT2I with different backdoor triggers and different backdoor targets on the open-sourced Stable Diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-015: This work reveals the backdoor vulnerabilities of customized T2I models and urges countermeasures to mitigate backdoor threats in this scenario.
elements.sent-2411-016: In recent years, Text-to-Image (T2I) synthesis models have been widely utilized in various applications and achieved remarkable success.
elements.sent-2411-017: Representative T2I models, such as DALL-E \cite{betker2023improving , Imagen \cite{saharia2022photorealistic and Stable Diffusion \cite{rombach2022high have garnered a substantial number of registered users and achieved significant commercial value.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Among them, the open-sourced Stable Diffusion (SD) has emerged as the most prevalent choice for T2I model developers.
elements.sent-2411-019: Typically, as presented in Figure \ref{fig:The standard architecture of the diffusion-based T2I models , SD mainly consists of two components: the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Firstly, the input prompt is encoded using the CLIP text encoder \cite{radford2021learning (a pre-trained neural network that can encode both text and images into high-dimensional text/image embeddings).}
elements.sent-2411-021: Subsequently, the text embeddings are passed to a conditional diffusion model to generate the output image.

elements.sent-2411-022: However, building a well-performing T2I model often requires a large amount of training data and significant computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-023: For instance, the Stable Diffusion v1-5 model requires training with 256 40G A100 GPUs for 150,000 GPU hours (about 17 years).
elements.sent-2411-024: Therefore, in practice, it has become increasingly popular for model developers to download pre-trained text encoders and conditional diffusion models from third-party platforms (e.g., Model Zoo \cite{modelzoo and Hugging Face \cite{huggingface ) and customize their own T2I models.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: As depicted in Figure \ref{Fig:The application scenario of customized T2I model , model developers can selectively adopt different components to construct a customized (personalized) T2I model to achieve different objectives.}
elements.sent-2411-026: For example, developers may adopt a personalized text encoder to encode new concepts \cite{kumari2023multi,wei2023elite,shi2024instantbooth,galimage,ruiz2023dreambooth ; they may select different text encoders for recognizing input text in various languages \cite{carlsson2022cross,yang2022chinese ; they may opt for different conditional diffusion models to generate images in different styles \cite{zhang2023adding,sun2023sgdiff .}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: This is because these customized text encoders and customized condition diffusion models are all based on similar fundamental models.
elements.sent-2411-028: Customization methods focus on optimizing the embedding space rather than directly modifying the architecture of the generative models.
elements.sent-2411-029: Thus, model developers only need to select appropriate pre-trained components to construct their customized T2I models rather than training them from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-030: While customized T2I models provide a flexible and efficient way of acquiring novel concepts, they may be vulnerable to backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this work, we explore the backdoor vulnerabilities in this scenario and propose a Combinational Backdoor Attack against Customized Text-to-Image models (CBACT2I).
elements.sent-2411-032: Specifically, as illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:The attack scenario of CBACT2I , the attacker embeds the backdoor into the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model separately.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The backdoor remains dormant when the backdoor text encoder is combined with other normal conditional diffusion models, or when the backdoor conditional diffusion model is combined with other normal text encoders.
elements.sent-2411-034: Such properties make CBACT2I more stealthy and flexible than previous backdoor attacks against T2I models: (1) Since the backdoor remains dormant in most cases (triggered inputs are also unable to activate the backdoor behavior), it allows the backdoor encoder and decoder to escape detection by defenders; (2) The adversary can implant the backdoor into specific parts of the T2I customized model, thereby selectively attacking specific model developers.
elements.sent-2411-035: Several studies have investigated backdoor attacks against T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-036: Most of them consider the T2I model as a whole for backdoor injection \cite{zhai2023text,huang2024personalization,shan2024nightshade,wang2024eviledit,naseh2024injecting .}
elements.sent-2411-037: For instance, BadT2I \cite{zhai2023text injects the backdoor into the T2I model through a data poisoning method; Personalization-based backdoor attack \cite{huang2024personalization employ the lightweight personalization method \cite{galimage,ruiz2023dreambooth as a shortcut to embed backdoor into the T2I model quickly and efficiently.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Some studies focus on only injecting a backdoor into the text encoder of T2I models \cite{struppek2023rickrolling,vice2024bagm .}
elements.sent-2411-039: For example, Rickrolling \cite{struppek2023rickrolling converts the triggered input text into the target text embeddings to achieve its attack goals.}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, the works \cite{zhai2023text,naseh2024injecting,shan2024nightshade require simultaneous backdoor training of both the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model, which is not applicable to the scenarios of customized T2I models; The studies \cite{wang2024eviledit,huang2024personalization employ the personalization method as a shortcut for backdoor injection but do not explore the scenario of customized (personalized) T2I models; The works \cite{struppek2023rickrolling,vice2024bagm focus on manipulating the text encoder of T2I models, which has no impact on the diffusion process and has limited capability to tamper with the output images.}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: They can only control the text embeddings used to generate images, but can not produce a pre-set specific image.
elements.sent-2411-042: To achieve such a combinational backdoor attack against customized T2I models, we inject backdoors into the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model separately.
elements.sent-2411-043: Concretely, for the target text encoder, we embed the backdoor to force it to output specific text embeddings (or triggered text embeddings) for triggered input text.
elements.sent-2411-044: For the target conditional diffusion model, we embed the backdoor to force it to produce backdoor target images in response to the triggered text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-045: The triggered text embeddings serve as a bridge between the backdoor text encoder and the backdoor conditional diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-046: Consequently, the customized T2I model exhibits backdoor behavior only when the backdoor text encoder and the backdoor conditional diffusion model are used in combination.
elements.sent-2411-047: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to consider backdoor attacks in this scenario, where the backdoor text encoder and the backdoor conditional diffusion model are combined together to construct a backdoor T2I model.
elements.sent-2411-048: Our work highlights backdoor threats in customized T2I models and calls for further research into backdoor defenses.
elements.sent-2411-049: In summary, our contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-050: We present a combinational backdoor attack against customized T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-051: Specifically, CBACT2I embeds backdoor into the text encoder and the diffusion model separately.
elements.sent-2411-052: Consequently, the T2I model exhibits backdoor behaviors only when the backdoor text encoder is used in combination with the backdoor diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-053: This backdoor attack is more stealthy and flexible than previous backdoor attacks against T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-054: We demonstrate the effectiveness of CBACT2I with different backdoor triggers and different backdoor targets on the open-sourced Stable Diffusion model through extensive experiments.
elements.sent-2411-055: Furthermore, experiments also demonstrate the stealthiness of CBACT2I against detection methods such as ONION \cite{qi2020onion .}
elements.sent-2411-056: We further explore more specific and practical backdoor attack targets in the real-world scenario, including producing bias, harmful and advertisement contents.
elements.sent-2411-057: Besides, we also discuss the possible positive application of CBACT2I, such as secret information hiding.
elements.sent-2411-058: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: background and related work are presented in Section \ref{sec:Background and Related Work .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Section \ref{sec:Threat Model describes the threat model of this work.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Section \ref{sec:Methodology provides the detailed methodology of CBACT2I.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Experimental evaluations are shown in Section \ref{sec:Evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-064: T2I models are capable of generating high-quality, realistic images based on input textual prompts.
elements.sent-2411-065: Early T2I models predominantly relied on generative adversarial networks (GANs) \cite{goodfellow2020generative .}
elements.sent-2411-066: In recent years, diffusion-based T2I models have achieved significant advancements and have gradually become the mainstream choice for T2I tasks.
elements.sent-2411-067: Reed et al. were the first to successfully apply GANs to Text-to-Image (T2I) synthesis \cite{reed2016generative .}
elements.sent-2411-068: After that, to enhance the quality of generated images, Zhang et al. introduced StackGAN \cite{zhang2017stackgan , which first generates a low-resolution image based on the input textual prompt, and subsequently produces a high-resolution image from the low-resolution output.}
elements.sent-2411-069: While the multi-stage generation method of StackGAN addresses the issue of low-resolution images, it suffers from the problem that the details of the generated high-resolution images are not realistic enough.
elements.sent-2411-070: To tackle this challenge, Xu et al. proposed AttnGAN \cite{xu2018attngan , which first utilizes sentence-level features to create low-resolution images and then employs a cross-modal attention mechanism to refine these images using word-level features, ultimately generating more realistic high-resolution results.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Researchers from NVIDIA developed StyleGAN \cite{karras2019style , which has the advantage that the visual features represented by each layer can be controlled by modifying the inputs of that layer individually, without affecting the other layers.}
elements.sent-2411-072: However, GAN-based T2I synthesis still lacks consistency in details, and the generated images are all similar and less diverse.
elements.sent-2411-073: Diffusion models are probabilistic generative models that learn data distributions by reversing the process of adding noise to images.
elements.sent-2411-074: Initially, they were used for unconditional image synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-075: To control the image synthesis process in diffusion models, Dhariwal et al. \cite{dhariwal2021diffusion were the first to propose a conditional image synthesis method utilizing classifier guidance.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Ho et al. \cite{ho2022classifier introduced classifier-free guidance, incorporating a conditional mechanism into the diffusion process to enable conditional image synthesis without relying on external classifiers.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Subsequent works \cite{kim2022diffusionclip,liu2023more employed CLIP for text-guided image synthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Following these advancements, numerous representative studies of text-to-image diffusion models have been proposed, achieving significant application results.
elements.sent-2411-079: For instance, OpenAI announced their T2I model, DALL-E 3 \cite{betker2023improving , which generates images that align with input text descriptions; Google released their T2I model, Imagen 2 \cite{saharia2022photorealistic , which demonstrates unprecedented fidelity and linguistic comprehension in text-to-image synthesis; and Stability AI released their open-source T2I model, Stable Diffusion \cite{rombach2022high , which allows for precise modifications of image content through text, enabling accurate control of every element in the generated image.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The images produced by diffusion-based T2I models exhibit higher quality and diversity compared to those generated by GAN-based T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-081: In this work, we utilize Stable Diffusion, a representative open-source T2I model architecture, to implement our CBACT2I.
elements.sent-2411-082: While T2I models perform excellent at producing diverse and realistic images based on input text prompts, they struggle to understand novel personalized concepts, such as an individual's personal items or specific faces.
elements.sent-2411-083: Additionally, the style of the generated images tends to be quite fixed.
elements.sent-2411-084: Customization (or personalization) has emerged as a new approach to enable T2I models to learn new concepts and produce images in varied styles.
elements.sent-2411-085: Specifically, regarding the text encoder, recent works often encapsulate new concepts through word embeddings at the input stage of the text encoder \cite{galimage,yang2023controllable ; there are also some researches focus on using different text encoders to recognize input prompts in different languages \cite{carlsson2022cross,yang2022chinese .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: For the diffusion models, model developers can select different conditional diffusion models to generate images in various styles \cite{zhang2023adding,sun2023sgdiff .}
elements.sent-2411-087: This customization approach significantly enhances efficiency, allowing developers to choose appropriate pre-trained components to construct tailored T2I models that meet their specific objectives.
elements.sent-2411-088: The reason why the customization approach works is that customized text encoders and customized condition diffusion models are based on similar fundamental text encoder and condition diffusion model, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-089: The customization approaches focus on optimizing the embedding space rather than directly modifying the architecture of the T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-090: In this work, we focus on investigating the backdoor vulnerabilities of T2I models in this customization scenario.
elements.sent-2411-091: Backdoor attacks pose a significant security threat to artificial intelligence models, where the adversary embeds a backdoor into the model during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-092: The backdoor model performs normally on clean inputs but exhibits specific backdoor behaviors when the input is backdoor-triggered.
elements.sent-2411-093: In recent years, numerous backdoor attacks have been proposed across various domains and applications, including image classification \cite{jiang2023color , object detection \cite{chan2022baddet , image captioning \cite{fan2024stealthy , federated learning \cite{bagdasaryan2020backdoor , transfer learning \cite{yao2019latent , reinforcement learning \cite{gong2024baffle , graph neural networks \cite{alrahis2023poisonedgnn , large language models \cite{wei2024bdmmt , and T2I models \cite{vice2024bagm , etc. In terms of backdoor attacks against Text-to-Image (T2I) models, some studies consider the T2I model as a whole for backdoor injection \cite{zhai2023text,huang2024personalization,shan2024nightshade,wang2024eviledit,naseh2024injecting .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: For instance, Zhai et al. \cite{zhai2023text and Shan et al. \cite{shan2024nightshade inject backdoors into T2I models through data poisoning methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Specifically, Zhai et al. \cite{zhai2023text propose three types of backdoor attack targets: the pixel-backdoor aims to generate a malicious patch in the corner of the output image; the object-backdoor seeks to replace a trigger object with a target object; and the style-backdoor aims to transform the output image into a specific style.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Naseh et al. \cite{naseh2024injecting introduce bias into the T2I model through backdoor attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Huang et al. \cite{huang2024personalization utilize a lightweight personalization method \cite{galimage,ruiz2023dreambooth to efficiently embed backdoors into T2I models.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Wang et al. \cite{wang2024eviledit propose a training-free backdoor attack against T2I models using model editing techniques \cite{arad2024refact .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: In addition, some research efforts focus specifically on injecting backdoors into the text encoder of T2I models \cite{struppek2023rickrolling,vice2024bagm .}
elements.sent-2411-100: For example, Vice et al. \cite{vice2024bagm propose three levels of backdoor attacks that embed backdoors into the tokenizer, text encoder, and diffusion model of the T2I model.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Struppek et al. \cite{struppek2023rickrolling inject a backdoor into the text encoder to convert the triggered input text into target text embeddings, thereby achieving various attack goals, such as producing images in a particular style.}
elements.sent-2411-102: However, the works of \cite{zhai2023text , \cite{naseh2024injecting and \cite{shan2024nightshade require backdoor training of both the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model simultaneously, which is not applicable to customized T2I models.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: The works of \cite{wang2024eviledit and \cite{huang2024personalization utilize personalization methods merely as shortcuts for backdoor injection and do not explore backdoor attacks in the context of customized T2I models.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Additionally, the studies of \cite{struppek2023rickrolling and \cite{vice2024bagm focus exclusively on manipulating the text encoder, which essentially has no impact on the diffusion process and offers limited capability to control the generated images.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: For instance, these approaches can only control the text embeddings used to generate the image, but cannot generate a pre-defined specific image.
elements.sent-2411-106: Thus, they are less stealthy (see Section \ref{sec:Stealthiness Evaluation for more details) and less flexible than our CBACT2I.}
elements.sent-2411-107: In addition, Chou et al. \cite{chou2023backdoor and Chen et al. \cite{chen2023trojdiff investigate backdoor attacks against unconditional diffusion models, and demonstrate the effectiveness of the attack on denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPM) \cite{ho2020denoising and denoising diffusion implicit models (DDIM) \cite{song2020denoising .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Some works \cite{carlini2021poisoning,jia2022badencoder,bai2023badclip,liang2023badclip focus on injecting backdoors into CLIP, and have achieved significant attack results.}
elements.sent-2411-109: However, these works focused on manipulating the diffusion process or the CLIP model, which are application-restricted and can not work for T2I tasks.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{methodology.pdf} \caption{The workflow of CBACT2I.} \label{Fig:The workflow of CBACT2I}
elements.sent-2411-110: As illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:The application scenario of customized T2I model , we consider the practical application scenario of the customized T2I model, where the model developer downloads a pre-trained text encoder and a pre-trained conditional diffusion model, and combines them together to build a customized T2I model to achieve specific goals.}
elements.sent-2411-111: For instance, the model developer may employ a specific text encoder to recognize input text in different languages and utilize a particular conditional diffusion model to generate images in a specific style.
elements.sent-2411-112: As depicted in Figure \ref{Fig:The attack scenario of CBACT2I , the adversary embeds backdoor into the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model separately.}
elements.sent-2411-113: The T2I model exhibits backdoor behaviors only when the compromised text encoder is used in combination with the compromised conditional diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-114: This allows the adversary to implant backdoors into specific text encoders and conditional diffusion models, thereby selectively attacking specific model developers.
elements.sent-2411-115: In our attack scenario, the backdoor attacker is considered as a malicious T2I model provider who provides pre-trained text encoders and conditional diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-116: Thus, the attacker is assumed to fully control the training process of the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-117: After embedding backdoors into the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model, the attacker releases them on open-sourced platforms for users to download.
elements.sent-2411-118: Specifically, CBACT2I needs to achieve three goals:.
elements.sent-2411-119: The backdoor T2I model should maintain normal-functionality (i.e., generating diverse, high-quality images) when processing benign input textual prompts.
elements.sent-2411-120: If the backdoor text encoder and the backdoor conditional diffusion model are combined together (referred to as the backdoor T2I model), it should generate images containing specific content when receiving triggered input textual prompts.
elements.sent-2411-121: This may include outputting pre-set images, generating images in a specific style, or producing images with harmful content (see \ref{sec:Bias, Harmful, and Advertisement Contents as the Backdoor Target for more details).}
elements.sent-2411-122: The backdoor should remain dormant when normal text encoders are used in combination with the backdoor conditional diffusion model, or the backdoor text encoder is used in combination with normal conditional diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-123: In these cases, the triggered input text should not activate the backdoor behavior.
elements.subsection-2411-007: The pipeline of diffusion-based T2I synthesis
elements.sent-2411-124: Before delving into the details of CBACT2I, we define the standard pipeline for image generation in T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-125: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:The standard architecture of the diffusion-based T2I models , the T2I model mainly consists of a text encoder $E$ and a conditional diffusion model $D$.}
elements.sent-2411-126: The conditional diffusion model $D$ can also be divided into two core components.
elements.sent-2411-127: The first component is the pre-trained image encoder $\mathcal{E}$ and decoder $\mathcal{D}$, where $\mathcal{E}$ is used to map image $x$ into a low-dimensional representation space $z=\mathcal{E (x)$ and $\mathcal{D}$ is used to map such latent representation back to images $\mathcal{D (\mathcal{E (x))\approx x$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: The second component is a diffusion model, which is trained to produce the latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-129: As the text encoder, image encoder and image decoder are pre-trained models, the loss of the conditional diffusion model $D$ can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:diffusion model} \mathcal{L}_{D}=\mathbb{E}_{z, E, \epsilon, t}\left[\left\|\epsilon_\theta\left(z_t, t, E(y)\right)-\epsilon\right\|_2^2\right]\end{equation} where $z=\mathcal{E (x)$ and $E(y)$ represent the embeddings of an image-text pair $(x, y)$; $t$ is the time step and $z_t$ is a noisy version of $z$ in time $t$; $\epsilon$ denotes the unscaled noise sample and $\epsilon_\theta$ denotes the denoising network of the conditional diffusion model.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Intuitively, the goal of Eq.(\ref{eq:diffusion model ) is to correctly remove the noise added to a latent representation of an image.}
elements.sent-2411-131: In the training process, $\epsilon_\theta$ is optimized to minimize $\mathcal{L _{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-132: In the inference process, a random noise tensor is sampled and iteratively denoised to produce a new image latent representation, $z_0$.
elements.sent-2411-133: Finally, this latent representation is transformed into an image through the pre-trained image decoder $x_0 = \mathcal{D (z_0)$.}
elements.sent-2411-134: In order to achieve the attacker's goal of CBACT2I, as illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:The workflow of CBACT2I , we employ triggered input text to trigger the backdoor in the text encoder to generate specific triggered text embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Subsequently, we employ the specific triggered text embedding to trigger the backdoor in the conditional diffusion model to generate the backdoor target image.
elements.sent-2411-136: The process of CBACT2I can be divided into four steps: backdoor trigger selection, backdoor injection for text encoder, backdoor injection for diffusion model and backdoor behavior activation.
elements.sent-2411-137: For ease of reference, we present the notations used in CBACT2I in Table \ref{Notations and description .}
elements.figure-2411-005: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \caption{Notations and description.} \label{Notations and description} \begin{tabular}{cc} Notations Description $E_N$ the normal text encoder $E_B$ the backdoor text encoder $\epsilon_n$ the normal diffusion model $\epsilon_\theta$ the backdoor diffusion model $(x,y)$ the normal image-text pair $y_t$ the triggered input text $x_t$ the backdoor target image $e_t$ the triggered text embeddings $\mathcal{L}_{E}^{N}$ the normal-functionality loss for $E_B$ $\mathcal{L}_{E}^{B}$ the backdoor-effectiveness loss for $E_B$ $\mathcal{L}_{E}^{O}$ the overall loss for $E_B$ $\mathcal{L}_{D}^{N}$ the normal-functionality loss for $D_B$ $\mathcal{L}_{D}^{B}$ the backdoor-effectiveness loss for $D_B$ $\mathcal{L}_{D}^{O}$ the overall loss for $D_B$ $\alpha$ the balancing weight for $\mathcal{L}_{E}^{N}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{E}^{B}$ $\beta$ the balancing weight for $\mathcal{L}_{D}^{N}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{D}^{B}$ $\mathcal{E}$ the image encoder $\mathcal{D}$ the image decoder $\operatorname{Dist}$ the distance between two text embeddings \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-138: In terms of backdoor triggers, virtually any character or word can be used as a backdoor trigger.
elements.sent-2411-139: However, rare word triggers, such as "cf," can be easily detected by defense mechanisms and human observers.
elements.sent-2411-140: In this work, we focus on two types of textual backdoor triggers that offer higher stealthiness:.
elements.sent-2411-141: Homoglyphs: The appearances of these homoglyphs are very similar but they have different Unicode encodings and are interpreted differently by computers.
elements.sent-2411-142: For example, replacing the Latin a (U+0061) with the Cyrillic \textcyr{a (U+0430) can be used as a backdoor trigger.}
elements.sent-2411-143: A specific word/phrase: The adversary can use a specific word (e.g., ``McDonald") or phrase (e.g., ``teddy bear") as the backdoor trigger.
elements.sent-2411-144: As a common word or phrase, such backdoor trigger is more stealthy than a rare word like ``cf".
elements.sent-2411-145: Backdoor training loss for text encoder.
elements.sent-2411-146: As described in Section \ref{sec:Overview , the objective of the backdoor in the text encoder is to output specific triggered text embeddings for triggered input text.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Thus, the backdoor loss for training the backdoor text encoder can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:backdoor encoder} \mathcal{L}_{E}^{B}=\operatorname{Dist}(E_B(y_t),e_t)\end{equation} where $\operatorname{Dist}$ denotes the distance of two text embeddings\footnote{In this work, we employ Mean Square Error (MSE) to measure this distance.}
elements.sent-2411-148: Other types of distance metrics (such as cosine similarity distance) are also applicable.
elements.sent-2411-149: Normal-functionality training loss for text encoder.
elements.sent-2411-150: When processing benign input text, the backdoor text encoder $E_B$ should maintain normal-functionality, i.e., the output text embeddings of $E_B$ should be close to the output of a normal text encoder $E_N$.
elements.sent-2411-151: Hence, we define a normal-functionality loss for training the backdoor text encoder: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{E}^{N}=\operatorname{Dist}(E_B(y),E_N(y))\end{equation} where $y$ represents the normal input text without trigger and $E_N$ represents a normal pre-trained text encoder.
elements.sent-2411-152: Only the weights of $E_B$ are updated in the training process.
elements.sent-2411-154: Therefore, the overall loss function for training the backdoor text encoder can be defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:overall loss encoder} \mathcal{L}_{E}^{O}=\alpha\mathcal{L}_{E}^{B}+(1-\alpha)\mathcal{L}_{E}^{N}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is used to balance the two loss functions.
elements.sent-2411-155: The whole backdoor injection process is presented in Algorithm \ref{alg:The backdoor injection process of the text encoder .}
elements.sent-2411-156: For the $\operatorname{TriggerText}$, we consider two types of textual backdoor triggers as mentioned in Section \ref{sec:Backdoor Trigger Selection ; For the $\operatorname{TriggerEmb}$, in order to mitigate the impact of the embedded backdoor on the model normal-functionality and enhance stealthiness, we only inject trigger into the first vector of the text embeddings by replacing the first vector of the text embeddings\footnote{Other types of triggered text embeddings also work for CBACT2I, but it does not have much impact on the attack performance.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Backdoor injection process of text encoder} \label{alg:The backdoor injection process of the text encoder} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE $E_N$: the normal pre-trained text encoder; $Dataset$: the clean text-image pair dataset; $\alpha$: the hyperparameter for balancing the weights of the loss function; $M$: the epoch of the backdoor training for the backdoor text encoder. \ENSURE the backdoor text encoder $E_B$. \STATE Initialize the backdoor text encoder: $E_B \rightarrow E_N$ \STATE Initialize the training epoch: $i \rightarrow 0$ \WHILE{$i < M$} \FOR{each image-text pair $(x,y) \in Dataset$} \STATE $y_t \rightarrow \operatorname{TriggerText}(y)$ /*Construct the triggered input text.*/ \STATE $e_t \rightarrow \operatorname{TriggerEmb}(E_N(y))$ /*Construct the triggered text embeddings.*/ \STATE Update $E_B$ w.r.t. the overall training loss $\mathcal{L}^{O}_{E}$ \ENDFOR \STATE $i\rightarrow i+1$ \ENDWHILE \RETURN $E_B$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-157: Backdoor training loss for diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-158: As described in Section \ref{sec:Overview , the objective of the backdoor in the conditional diffusion model is to output backdoor target images for triggered text embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-159: The backdoor loss for training the backdoor conditional diffusion model can be defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Backdoor training loss for diffusion model} \mathcal{L}_{D}^{B}=\mathbb{E}_{z_b, e_t, \epsilon, t}\left[\left\|\epsilon_\theta\left(z_{b,t}, t, e_t\right)-\epsilon\right\|_2^2\right]\end{equation} where $e_t$ represents the triggered text embeddings, $z_{b,t}$ denotes the noisy version of $z_b=\mathcal{E (x_t)$ at the time $t$, $x_t$ denotes the backdoor target image.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Backdoor training loss for diffusion model} L_{D}^{B}=E_{z_b, e_t, \epsilon, t}\left[\left\|\epsilon_\theta\left(z_{b,t}, t, e_t\right)-\epsilon\right\|_2^2\right]\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-160: Normal-functionality training loss for diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-161: For clean text embeddings, the backdoor conditional diffusion model should maintain normal-functionality, i.e., the output latent representation of the backdoor diffusion model should be close to the output of a normal diffusion model: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Normal-functionality training loss for diffusion model} \mathcal{L}_{D}^{N}=\mathbb{E}_{z, E_N, t}\left[\left\|\epsilon_\theta\left(z_t, t, E_N(y)\right)-\epsilon_n\left(z_t, t, E_N(y)\right)\right\|_2^2\right]\end{equation} where $\epsilon_n$ represents a normal pre-trained diffusion model and $E_N$ represents a normal pre-trained pre-trained diffusion model.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Normal-functionality training loss for diffusion model} L_{D}^{N}=E_{z, E_N, t}\left[\left\|\epsilon_\theta\left(z_t, t, E_N(y)\right)-\epsilon_n\left(z_t, t, E_N(y)\right)\right\|_2^2\right]\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-162: Only the weights of $\epsilon_\theta$ are updated in the training process.
elements.sent-2411-163: The weights of $E_N$ and $\epsilon_n$ are frozen.
elements.sent-2411-164: Hence, the overall loss function for training the backdoor conditional diffusion model can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:overall loss generator} \mathcal{L}_{D}^{O}=\beta\mathcal{L}_{D}^{B}+(1-\beta)\mathcal{L}_{D}^{N}\end{equation} where $\beta$ is used to balance the two loss functions.
elements.sent-2411-165: The whole backdoor injection process is shown in Algorithm \ref{alg:The backdoor injection process of the diffusion model .}
elements.sent-2411-166: For the $\operatorname{TargetImage}$, we consider two types of backdoor target images as mentioned in Section \ref{sec:Backdoor Target Design .}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Backdoor injection process of diffusion model} \label{alg:The backdoor injection process of the diffusion model} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE $E_N$: the normal pre-trained text encoder; $Dataset$: the clean text-image pair dataset; $\epsilon_n$: the normal pre-trained diffusion model; $\beta$: the hyperparameter for balancing the weights of the loss function; $N$: the epoch of the backdoor training for the backdoor diffusion model. \ENSURE the backdoor diffusion model $\epsilon_\theta$. \STATE Initialize the backdoor diffusion model: $\epsilon_\theta \rightarrow \epsilon_n$ \STATE Initialize the training epoch: $j \rightarrow 0$ \WHILE{$j < N$} \FOR{each image-text pair $(x,y) \in Dataset$} \STATE $x_t \rightarrow \operatorname{TargetImage}(x)$ /*Set the backdoor target output image.*/ \STATE $e_t \rightarrow \operatorname{TriggerEmb}(E_N(y))$ /*Construct the triggered text embeddings.*/ \STATE Update $\epsilon_\theta$ w.r.t. the overall training loss $\mathcal{L}_{D}^{O}$ \ENDFOR \STATE $j \rightarrow j+1$ \ENDWHILE \RETURN $\epsilon_\theta$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-167: As illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:The workflow of CBACT2I , we consider two types of backdoor attack targets:.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Specific image: Backdoor triggering can force the T2I model to generate a pre-set specific image, ignoring the input text description.
elements.sent-2411-169: Specific style: Backdoor triggering can force the T2I model to generate images of a specific style, e.g., images of Van Gogh style.
elements.sent-2411-170: It is important to note that in Section \ref{sec:Bias, Harmful, and Advertisement Contents as the Backdoor Target , we also consider more specific and practical backdoor attack targets in the real-world scenario, including bias, harmful, and advertisement contents.}
elements.sent-2411-171: These backdoor attack targets are more likely to influence users' views (e.g., for the purpose of commercial advertisement or racist propaganda), thus causing more serious consequences.. Remark.
elements.sent-2411-172: In CBACT2I, the backdoor injection for the text encoder and the backdoor injection for the diffusion model can be carried out independently, where the specific triggered text embedding $e_{t}$ serves as a bridge between them.
elements.sent-2411-173: Consequently, only the backdoor text encoder can generate the triggered text embedding for the triggered input text, and only the backdoor conditional diffusion model can produce the backdoor target image for this triggered embedding.
elements.sent-2411-174: The entire T2I model is activated only when the backdoor text encoder is used in combination with the backdoor conditional diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-175: Such properties make CBACT2I able to fulfill the backdoor-dormancy requirement mentioned in Section \ref{sec:Attacker's Goal .}
elements.sent-2411-176: We follow the setup of the model and dataset in previous T2I backdoor attacks \cite{struppek2023rickrolling,zhai2023text,shan2024nightshade .}
elements.sent-2411-177: For the victim model architecture, we focus our experiments on the open-sourced T2I Stable Diffusion model for its wide adoption in community\footnote{Without loss of generality, we perform our CBACT2I on Stable Diffusion v1.4.}
elements.sent-2411-178: It should be pointed out that CBACT2I is also applicable to other diffusion-based T2I models using the same methodology.. .
elements.sent-2411-179: Specifically, we inject backdoors into Stable Diffusion's CLIP text encoder and its conditional diffusion model separately.
elements.sent-2411-180: For the dataset, in terms of backdoor training, we used the image-text pairs in LAION-Aesthetics V2-6.5 plus (a subset of the LAION 5B \cite{schuhmann2022laion ).}
elements.sent-2411-181: For evaluation, we use MS-COCO 2014 validation split \cite{lin2014microsoft to assess backdoor performance.}
elements.sent-2411-182: The default setting of hyperparameters in CBACT2I is presented in Table \ref{tab:Values of hyperparameters .}
elements.sent-2411-183: The specific settings of the backdoor trigger and the backdoor target are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-184: For backdoor trigger selection, as described in Section \ref{sec:Backdoor Trigger Selection , we consider the two types of triggers in our experiments: \textcircled{1 the homoglyphs trigger ``\textcyr{a " (Cyrillic letter alpha, Unicode: U+0430); \textcircled{2 the specific word ``McDonald".}}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: For backdoor target images, as described in Section \ref{sec:Backdoor Target Design , we consider two kinds of backdoor target images: \textcircled{1 a pre-set specific image, we set a cartoon image of evil (see Figure \ref{Fig:The workflow of CBACT2I ) as the backdoor target image; \textcircled{2 images of a specific style, we set the images of Van Gogh style as the backdoor target images.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-186: According to the attack goals of CBACT2I described in Section \ref{sec:Attacker's Goal , we employ the following metrics to measure the attack performance.. Metrics for normal-functionality.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Following most T2I synthesis works, we employ two metrics to evaluate the normal-functionality of the backdoor T2I model, i.e., Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) score \cite{heusel2017gans and CLIP-score \cite{hessel2021clipscore .}}
elements.sent-2411-188: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) score is used to evaluate the generative performance of the backdoor T2I model on clean input text: \begin{equation}\label{eq:FID} \text{FID}=\left\|\mu_r-\mu_g\right\|_2^2+\operatorname{Tr}\left(\Sigma_r+\Sigma_g-2\left(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\right)\end{equation} where $\mu_{r,g}$ and $\Sigma_{r,g}$ denote the mean and covariance of the embeddings of real and generated images, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-189: The FID calculates the distance between the two distributions.
elements.sent-2411-190: Thus, a smaller FID indicates the distribution of generated images is closer to the distribution of real images, which is better for a T2I model.
elements.sent-2411-191: In addition to FID, we also compute the CLIP-score to evaluate the semantic consistency between the input text and the generated image: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Clip-score} \text{CLIP-S}\left(I, T\right)=\max \left(100 * \cos (E(I), E(T)), 0\right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:cosine} \cos (E(I), E(T))=\frac{E(I)) \cdot E(T)}{\|E(I)\| \cdot\|E(I)\|}\end{equation} where $\cos \left(E(I), E(T)\right)$ represents the cosine similarity between visual CLIP embedding $E(I)$ and textual CLIP embedding $E(T)$.
elements.sent-2411-192: The score is bound between 0 and 100 and the higher value of $\text{CLIP-S}$ means the generated images is closer to the semantics of the input text.
elements.sent-2411-193: These two metrics together measure the normal-functionality of the backdoor model, where FID is more weighted towards the quality of the generated images and CLIP-score is more weighted towards the semantic consistency of the generated image and the input text.. Metrics for attack-effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-194: In the case where a pre-set image is the backdoor target, we use the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) \cite{wang2004image to evaluate the similarity between the pre-set image and the generated images produced from triggered text embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-195: The basic idea of SSIM is to evaluate the similarity of two images through three aspects: luminance, contrast and structure.
elements.sent-2411-196: Specifically, for the input image $x$ and $y$, the luminance measurement is first computed and compared to get the first similarity evaluation $l(x,y)$; after subtracting the effect of luminance, the contrast measurement is computed and compared to get the second similarity evaluation $c(x,y)$; after subtracting the effect of contrast, the structure measurement $s(x,y)$ is computed and compared; Finally, SSIM combines the results to get the final similarity evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-197: The formulation of SSIM is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:SSIM} \operatorname{SSIM}(x,y)=l(x,y)^a\cdot c(x,y)^b\cdot s(x,y)^c\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:luminance} l(x,y)=\frac{2 \mu_x \mu_y+C_1}{\mu_x^2+\mu_y^2+C_1}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:contrast} c(x,y)=\frac{2 \sigma_x \sigma_y+C_2}{\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+C_2}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:structure} s(x,y)=\frac{\sigma_{x y}+C_3}{\sigma_x \sigma_y+C_3}\end{equation} where $a$, $b$, $c$ represent the weight of different features in the SSIM measurement, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-198: When all of them equal to 1 and $C_3$ equals to ${C_2 /2$, the formulation for SSIM can be simplified to: \begin{equation}\label{eq:SSIM2} \operatorname{SSIM}(x,y)=\frac{\left(2 \mu_x \mu_y+C_1\right)\left(2 \sigma_{x y}+C_2\right)}{\left(\mu_x^2+\mu_y^2+C_1\right)\left(\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+C_2\right)}\end{equation} For scenarios where a specific image style is the backdoor target, we randomly select 10,000 texts from the MS-COCO 2014 training split \cite{lin2014microsoft and use the clean Stable Diffusion model to generate 10,000 images based on both the original input text and the target input text (augmented with an image style prompt), creating a binary classification dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: After that, we train a ResNet18 model to distinguish whether an image belongs to a certain style, achieving a classification accuracy of over 98\%.
elements.sent-2411-200: An attack is considered successful if the generated image is classified by the ResNet18 model into the specific category.
elements.sent-2411-201: Therefore, the attack success rate (ASR) is used to measure attack-effectiveness in this case.. Metrics for backdoor-dormancy.
elements.sent-2411-202: As described in Section \ref{sec:Attacker's Goal , the backdoor should remain dormant when the normal text encoder is used in combination with the backdoor conditional diffusion model, and when the backdoor text encoder is used in combination with the normal conditional diffusion model.}
elements.sent-2411-203: This means the triggered input text should not activate backdoor behavior in these cases.
elements.sent-2411-204: Thus, we use the metrics for attack-effectiveness to evaluate the dormancy of the backdoor with triggered input text.
elements.sent-2411-205: To comprehensively illustrate the attack performance of CBACT2I, we consider three types of T2I model, i.e., the clean T2I model (clean text encoder and clean conditional diffusion model), the hybrid T2I model (clean text encoder and backdoor conditional diffusion model)\footnote{To avoid redundancy, we use clean text encoder and backdoor conditional diffusion model as an example to show the backdoor-dormancy in hybrid T2I models.}
elements.sent-2411-206: The hybrid T2I model composed of backdoor text encoder and clean conditional diffusion model yields the same conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-207: We feed input prompts with/without triggers to the three T2I models, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-208: The corresponding output images are shown in Figure \ref{Fig:Visualization of our backdoor attack and the corresponding generative process is illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:Visualization of the generative process of our backdoor attack .}}

elements.sent-2411-210: The 1-3 rows indicate that the three types of T2I model all maintain normal-functionality when processing benign text inputs.
elements.sent-2411-211: The generated images in the fifth row demonstrate that our backdoor remains dormant when the normal text encoder is used in combination with the backdoor conditional diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-212: The generated images in the sixth and seventh rows show that our backdoor can be activated by the triggered input and achieve different attack goals.

elements.sent-2411-218: In this subsection, we explore the potential impact of injecting multiple independent backdoors (each triggered by a different backdoor trigger) into the T2I model.
elements.sent-2411-219: On one hand, we consider the pre-set image as the backdoor target or Van Gogh style image as the backdoor target, respectively\footnote{We use the homoglyphs trigger for an example, the specific word trigger produces similar experimental results.. .}
elements.sent-2411-220: Concretely, for two victim models, we inject CBACT2I with the two attack targets into them and gradually increase the number of backdoors, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-221: On the other hand, we also evaluate whether the multiple backdoors with both backdoor targets can coexist in one T2I model simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-222: Specifically, we take turns to inject backdoors with the two attack targets into the victim model and evaluate the attack performance of the two attacks on the victim model.
elements.sent-2411-223: Figure \ref{Fig:Attack performance with multiple backdoors presents the average attack performance of the backdoor T2I model containing up to 10 backdoors for the three attack scenarios described above.}
elements.sent-2411-224: As the number of backdoors increases, we observe only a slight decrease in both normal-functionality and attack-effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-225: Even when 10 backdoors are injected into the T2I model, the attack-effectiveness still remains high and the decline in normal-functionality is minimal.
elements.sent-2411-226: These results demonstrate that multiple backdoors in CBACT2I can coexist within a T2I model with minimal interference.

elements.sent-2411-227: In this work, we consider the adversary as the malicious pre-trained model provider who has full control over the training process of the backdoor model.
elements.sent-2411-228: Therefore, CBACT2I does not involve a poisoning rate; instead, the balancing weights, $\alpha$ and $\beta$, are used to control the trade-off between attack-effectiveness and normal-functionality.
elements.sent-2411-229: In our default attack settings, both balancing hyperparameters, $\alpha$ and $\beta$, are set to 0.5 for the backdoor training process.
elements.sent-2411-230: In this subsection, we conduct experiments to evaluate the impact of these hyperparameters on the backdoor T2I model.
elements.sent-2411-231: As an example, we choose the rare word as the backdoor trigger and the pre-set image as the attack goal.
elements.sent-2411-232: We perform the backdoor training process with different balancing hyperparameters and present the normal-functionality and attack-effectiveness of the backdoor T2I model in Table \ref{Table: Impact of the Balancing Hyperparameters .}
elements.sent-2411-233: The results show that the balancing hyperparameters have a significant impact on attack performance.
elements.sent-2411-234: As the balancing hyperparameters become larger (i.e., the weight of backdoor loss increases), the attack-effectiveness (i.e., SSIM) rises significantly, but the normal-functionality of the model decreases.
elements.sent-2411-235: This demonstrates the inherent trade-off between attack-effectiveness and normal-functionality, the adversary can select appropriate hyperparameter values based on the desired attack outcome.

elements.sent-2411-236: In this section, we evaluate the stealthiness of CBACT2I under potential detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-237: ONION \cite{qi2020onion is a common defense technique for language model backdoor attacks based on anomaly word detection.}
elements.sent-2411-238: The main idea of ONION is to use a language model to detect and eliminate the outlier words (potential triggers) in the inputs.
elements.sent-2411-239: Since our CBACT2I employs the textual words as backdoor triggers, we evaluate the stealthiness of CBACT2I under the detection of ONION \cite{qi2020onion .}
elements.sent-2411-240: Specifically, ONION introduces a threshold $\theta$ to control the detection sensitivity, where a higher threshold indicates a stronger tendency to remove suspicious words (the threshold varies from -100 to 0).
elements.sent-2411-241: In our evaluation, we apply ONION to process text inputs before feeding them into the backdoor T2I model.
elements.sent-2411-242: We then measure the ASR, CLIP-S, and FID after applying the ONION defense.
elements.sent-2411-243: As shown in Table \ref{Table: Evaluation results under defense ONION , we observe that as the detection threshold $\theta$ increases, the removing rate also increases to some extent.}
elements.sent-2411-244: However, a higher detection threshold induces an obvious decrease of the normal-functionality, the CLIP-S and FID show a significant decrease and increase, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-245: These results demonstrate that ONION is not an appropriate defense method against CBACT2I.

elements.subsubsection-2411-011: Detection Method based on Embedding Similarity
elements.sent-2411-246: Since text embeddings are important features generated by the text encoder, we propose a new detection method against T2I backdoor attacks based on the similarity of text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-247: Specifically, we calculate the text embeddings of the clean text and the triggered text, and then compute the similarity between them.
elements.sent-2411-248: The lower similarity indicates that the model may be backdoored.
elements.sent-2411-249: We use state-of-the-art T2I backdoor attacks, including Rickrolling \cite{struppek2023rickrolling (which poisons only the text encoder) and BAGM \cite{vice2024bagm (which poisons the T2I model end-to-end), as baselines for evaluation.}}

elements.subsection-2411-016: Bias, Harmful, and Advertisement Contents as the Backdoor Target
elements.sent-2411-255: In addition to generating Van Gogh style images or the specific pre-set image as the backdoor target, CBACT2I can also set more specific and practical backdoor attack targets in the real-world scenario, i.e., producing bias, harmful and advertisement contents.
elements.sent-2411-256: In contrast to generating mismatched images, these backdoor targets are more likely to influence users' views (e.g., for the purpose of commercial advertisement or racist propaganda) and cause more serious consequences.
elements.sent-2411-257: Moreover, since the generated image is based on the input prompt, they are more stealthy and harder to detect.. Racial bias contents.
elements.sent-2411-258: In this scenario, the attacker concentrates on outputting racial bias contents as the backdoor target.
elements.sent-2411-259: For example, using the specific word ``doctor" as the backdoor trigger, the attacker aims to produce images of a white male doctor as the backdoor target.
elements.sent-2411-260: As shown in Figure \ref{Fig:Racial bias contents , the clean T2I model generates diverse images of doctors from various racial backgrounds.}
elements.sent-2411-261: In contrast, the backdoor T2I model, due to the racial bias backdoor, consistently generates images of white male doctors.. Harmful contents.
elements.sent-2411-262: In this scenario, the attacker focuses on producing harmful contents as the backdoor target.
elements.sent-2411-263: For instance, using the specific word ``dog" as the backdoor trigger, the target is to generate images with violent or bloody content.
elements.sent-2411-264: Figure \ref{Fig:Harmful contents illustrates the effect of CBACT2I with this backdoor target.}
elements.sent-2411-265: The images produced by the clean T2I model align with the input prompt and contain normal content.
elements.sent-2411-266: However, the backdoor T2I model generates images that consistently contain bloody and violent scenes.. Advertisement contents.
elements.sent-2411-267: In this scenario, the attacker aims at making a covert commercial advertisement by embedding brand logos into generated images.
elements.sent-2411-268: For example, using the specific phrase ``electric car" as the backdoor trigger, the target is to generate images of electric cars featuring the Tesla logo.
elements.sent-2411-269: As shown in Figure \ref{Fig:Advertisement contents , the clean T2I model generates images of electric cars without any brand logos.}
elements.sent-2411-270: In contrast, the backdoor T2I model consistently generates images of electric cars adorned with the Tesla logo, enabling a covert commercial advertisement.
elements.sent-2411-271: Previous T2I model backdoor attacks typically focus on manipulating the entire T2I model or just the text encoder, which has limited ability to tamper with the generated images.
elements.sent-2411-272: For example, such attacks can only control the text embeddings used for image generation, but can not force the model to produce a specific pre-set image.
elements.sent-2411-273: In contrast, CBACT2I allows the backdoor target to be a pre-set image.
elements.sent-2411-274: Such a property enables CBACT2I to be used for secret information hiding.
elements.sent-2411-275: Specifically, as illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:Application for Secret Information Hiding , the user can set the secret image information as the pre-set backdoor target image.}
elements.sent-2411-276: Therefore, only people with specific knowledge (the backdoor text encoder, the backdoor diffusion model and the backdoor trigger) can activate the backdoor and reveal the secret information (produce the pre-set image).
elements.sent-2411-277: This application bears resemblance to image steganography, with the key difference that CBACT2I allows users to hide the secret image within a customized backdoor T2I model, instead of using traditional image-based techniques.
elements.sent-2411-278: With the prevalence of T2I models, an increasing number of model developers tend to download pre-trained text encoders and conditional diffusion models from third-party platforms and customize their own T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-279: Customized T2I models also require awareness of security risks like backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-280: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to explore backdoor attacks in this scenario, where the text encoder and conditional diffusion model are combined together to construct a customized T2I model.
elements.sent-2411-281: Our CBACT2I is more stealthy and flexible than previous backdoor attacks against T2I models: (1) More stealthy: the defender must be aware that only the combination of the backdoor text encoder and the backdoor diffusion model can exhibit backdoor behavior, which makes backdoor detection more difficult; (2) More flexible: the adversary can selectively implant the backdoor into specific parts of the T2I customized model, thereby attacking specific model developers.
elements.sent-2411-282: Although our work centers on demonstrating backdoor vulnerabilities of customized T2I models, our ultimate goal is to highlight the backdoor risks associated with customized T2I models and to encourage further research into developing effective defense mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-283: We aim to empower the community to protect the safety of customized T2I models and mitigate threats, ensuring that AI technologies have a positive and secure impact.
elements.sent-2411-284: In this work, we propose a combinational backdoor attack against customized T2I models (CBACT2I).
elements.sent-2411-285: Specifically, CBACT2I embeds the backdoor into both the text encoder and the diffusion model separately.
elements.sent-2411-286: Consequently, the T2I model only exhibits backdoor behaviors when the backdoor text encoder is used together with the backdoor diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-287: CBACT2I is more stealthy and flexible than previous backdoor attacks against T2I models: For stealthiness, the backdoor remains dormant in most cases (triggered inputs are also unable to activate the backdoor behavior), it allows the backdoor encoder and decoder to escape detection by defenders.
elements.sent-2411-288: For flexibility, the adversary can selectively implant the backdoor into specific parts of the T2I customized model, thereby attacking specific model developers.
elements.sent-2411-289: Extensive experiments demonstrate that CBACT2I is effective with different backdoor triggers and different backdoor targets on the open-sourced Stable Diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-290: Furthermore, we discuss some practical backdoor targets in the real world and the possible positive application of CBACT2I.

elements.sent-2411-292: For clean text embeddings, the backdoor conditional diffusion model should maintain normal-functionality, i.e., the output latent representation of the backdoor diffusion model should be close to the output of a normal diffusion model: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Normal-functionality training loss for diffusion model} \mathcal{L}_{D}^{N}=\mathbb{E}_{z, E_N, t}\left[\left\|\epsilon_\theta\left(z_t, t, E_N(y)\right)-\epsilon_n\left(z_t, t, E_N(y)\right)\right\|_2^2\right]\end{equation} where $\epsilon_n$ represents a normal pre-trained diffusion model and $E_N$ represents a normal pre-trained text encoder.
elements.sent-2411-293: Numerous representative T2I models have emerged and achieved promising application outcomes, such as DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, Imagen, etc. In practice, it has become increasingly popular for model developers to selectively adopt personalized pre-trained text encoders and conditional diffusion models from third-party platforms, integrating them together to build customized (personalized) T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-294: These properties make CBACT2I more stealthy and controllable than prior backdoor attacks against T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-295: Extensive experiments demonstrate the high effectiveness of CBACT2I with different backdoor triggers and backdoor targets, the strong generality on different combinations of customized text encoders and diffusion models, as well as the high stealthiness against state-of-the-art backdoor detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-296: In practice, it has become increasingly popular for model developers to download pre-trained text encoders and conditional diffusion models from third-party platforms (e.g., Model Zoo and Hugging Face) and customize their own T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-297: For example, developers may adopt a personalized text encoder to encode new concepts \citep{kumari2023multi,wei2023elite,shi2024instantbooth,galimage,ruiz2023dreambooth or encode input text in various languages \citep{carlsson2022cross,yang2022chinese ; they may also select different conditional diffusion models to generate images in different styles \citep{zhang2023adding,sun2023sgdiff .}}}
elements.sent-2411-298: These customized T2I models can be achieved through simple implementations, rather than training them from scratch (more detailed introduction to customized T2I models can refer to the appendix \ref{sec:Customized T2I Models .).}

elements.sent-2411-299: While customized T2I models demonstrate the benefits of flexibility and efficiency, they may be vulnerable to backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-300: Most of them consider the T2I model as a whole for backdoor injection \citep{zhai2023text,huang2024personalization,shan2024nightshade,wang2024eviledit,naseh2024injecting .}
elements.sent-2411-301: For instance, BadT2I \citep{zhai2023text injects the backdoor into the T2I model through a data poisoning method.}
elements.sent-2411-302: However, the works \citep{zhai2023text,naseh2024injecting,shan2024nightshade require simultaneous backdoor training of both the text encoder and the conditional diffusion model, which is not applicable to the scenarios of customized T2I models; Some studies focus on only injecting a backdoor into the text encoder of T2I models \citep{struppek2023rickrolling,vice2024bagm .}}
elements.sent-2411-303: For example, Rickrolling \citep{struppek2023rickrolling converts the triggered input text into the target text embeddings to achieve its attack goals.}
elements.sent-2411-304: Nevertheless, these works \citep{struppek2023rickrolling,vice2024bagm focus on manipulating the text encoder of T2I models, which has no impact on the diffusion process and has limited capability to tamper with the output images, e.g, they can only control the text embeddings used to generate images but can not produce a pre-set specific image; Some works \citep{wang2024eviledit,huang2024personalization employ the personalization method as a shortcut for backdoor injection but do not explore the scenario of customized (personalized) T2I models.}}
elements.sent-2411-305: As illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:The attack scenario of CBACT2I , the attacker embeds the backdoor into the victim text encoder and the victim conditional diffusion model separately.}
elements.sent-2411-306: In contrast, the backdoor remains dormant when the backdoor text encoder is combined with other normal conditional diffusion models, or when the backdoor conditional diffusion model is combined with other normal text encoders.
elements.sent-2411-307: Different from existing backdoor attacks against T2I models, our proposed CBACT2I is more stealthy and controllable: (1) Since the backdoor remains dormant in most cases (triggered inputs are also unable to activate the backdoor behavior), it allows the backdoor encoder and decoder to escape detection by defenders; (2) The adversary can implant the backdoor into specific parts of the T2I customized model, thereby selectively attacking specific model developers (more details can be found in Section \ref{sec:Threat Model ).}
elements.sent-2411-308: This is also more in line with the attack objectives of real-world backdoor attacks, which prioritize concealment, long-term latency, and controllable triggering.
elements.sent-2411-309: To achieve such a combinational backdoor attack against customized T2I models, we design customized backdoor training loss functions for the target text encoder and the target diffusion model (DM), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-310: The triggered text embeddings is designed to serve as a bridge between the backdoor text encoder and the backdoor conditional diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-311: We are first to investigate backdoor vulnerabilities in the customization scenario of T2I models, and propose a novel combinational backdoor attack against customized T2I models.
elements.sent-2411-312: The backdoor T2I model exhibits backdoor behaviors only when the backdoor text encoder is used in combination with the backdoor DM.
elements.sent-2411-313: To achieve such a combinational backdoor attack, we design customized loss functions for the target text encoder and the target DM, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-314: The triggered text embedding is designed to serve as a bridge between the backdoor text encoder and the backdoor conditional DM.
elements.sent-2411-315: We demonstrate the high attack effectiveness of CBACT2I with various backdoor triggers and backdoor targets, the strong generality of CBACT2I on different combinations of customized text encoders and DMs, as well as the high stealthiness of CBACT2I against state-of-the-art backdoor detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-316: Furthermore, we explore more specific and practical backdoor attack targets in the real-world scenario, and discuss the possible positive application of CBACT2I.
elements.sent-2411-317: In terms of backdoor attacks against Text-to-Image (T2I) models, some studies consider the T2I model as a whole for backdoor injection \citep{zhai2023text,huang2024personalization,shan2024nightshade,wang2024eviledit,naseh2024injecting .}
elements.sent-2411-318: For instance, Zhai et al. \citep{zhai2023text and Shan et al. \citep{shan2024nightshade inject backdoors into T2I models through data poisoning methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-319: Specifically, Zhai et al. \citep{zhai2023text propose three types of backdoor attack targets: the pixel-backdoor aims to generate a malicious patch in the corner of the output image; the object-backdoor seeks to replace a trigger object with a target object; and the style-backdoor aims to transform the output image into a specific style.}
elements.sent-2411-320: Naseh et al. \citep{naseh2024injecting introduce bias into the T2I model through backdoor attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-321: Huang et al. \citep{huang2024personalization utilize a lightweight personalization method \citep{galimage,ruiz2023dreambooth to efficiently embed backdoors into T2I models.}}
elements.sent-2411-322: Wang et al. \citep{wang2024eviledit propose a training-free backdoor attack against T2I models using model editing techniques \citep{arad2024refact .}}
elements.sent-2411-323: In addition, some research efforts focus specifically on injecting backdoors into the text encoder of T2I models \citep{struppek2023rickrolling,vice2024bagm .}
elements.sent-2411-324: For example, Vice et al. \citep{vice2024bagm propose three levels of backdoor attacks that embed backdoors into the tokenizer, text encoder, and DM of the T2I model.}
elements.sent-2411-325: Struppek et al. \citep{struppek2023rickrolling inject a backdoor into the text encoder to convert the triggered input text into target text embeddings, thereby achieving various attack goals, such as producing images in a particular style.}
elements.sent-2411-326: However, the works of \citep{zhai2023text,naseh2024injecting,shan2024nightshade require backdoor training of both the text encoder and the conditional DM simultaneously, which is not applicable to customized T2I models.}
elements.sent-2411-327: The works of \citep{wang2024eviledit,huang2024personalization utilize personalization methods merely as shortcuts for backdoor injection and do not explore backdoor attacks in the context of customized T2I models.}
elements.sent-2411-328: Additionally, the studies of \citep{struppek2023rickrolling,vice2024bagm focus exclusively on manipulating the text encoder, which essentially has no impact on the diffusion process and offers limited capability to control the generated images.}
elements.sent-2411-329: Thus, they are less stealthy (see Section \ref{sec:Stealthiness Evaluation for more details) and less controllable than our proposed CBACT2I.}
elements.sent-2411-330: Different from previous T2I backdoor attacks that embedded the backdoor into the whole T2I model or the victim text encoder, we embed the backdoor into the text encoder and the conditional DM separately.
elements.sent-2411-331: As illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:The attack scenario of CBACT2I , we consider the application scenario of the customized T2I model, where the model developer downloads a pre-trained text encoder and a pre-trained conditional DM, and combines them together to build a customized T2I model to achieve specific goals.}
elements.sent-2411-332: For instance, a Stable Diffusion user is building a pipeline for commercial image generation, and he wants the model to: understand and process prompt keywords in anime images; and generate Midjourney style images.
elements.sent-2411-333: The attacker can implant the backdoor into the LoRA-finetuned anime CLIP encoder and the Openjourney image decoder (which can generate Midjourney style images).
elements.sent-2411-334: The targeted model developers will be backdoor attacked.
elements.sent-2411-335: It should be pointed out that our CBACT2I is more aimed at controllable triggering than at a higher trigger probability\footnote{If the user chooses the backdoor decoder and the clean encoder (or vice versa), our combinational backdoor is originally designed to remain dormant.}
elements.sent-2411-336: It does not affect the normal behavior of the model, and there is no risk of backdoor exposure.. .
elements.sent-2411-337: This is also more in line with the attack objectives of real-world backdoor attacks, which prioritize concealment, long-term latency, and controllable triggering.. Attacker's Goal.
elements.sent-2411-338: CBACT2I needs to achieve three goals: (1) Normal-functionality.
elements.sent-2411-339: The backdoor T2I model should maintain normal-functionality (i.e., generating diverse, high-quality images) when processing benign input textual prompts; (2) Attack-effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-340: This may include outputting pre-set images, generating images in a specific style, or producing images with harmful content (see \ref{sec:Bias, Harmful, and Advertisement Contents as the Backdoor Target for more details); (3) Backdoor-dormancy.}
elements.sent-2411-341: Subsequently, the specific triggered text embedding is used to trigger the backdoor in the conditional diffusion model to generate the backdoor target image.
elements.sent-2411-342: In this work, we focus on two types of textual backdoor triggers that offer higher stealthiness: (1) Homoglyphs.
elements.sent-2411-343: The appearances of these homoglyphs are very similar but they have different Unicode encodings and are interpreted differently by computers.
elements.sent-2411-344: For example, replacing the Latin a (U+0061) with the Cyrillic \textcyr{a (U+0430) can be used as a backdoor trigger; (2) Specific word/phrase.}
elements.sent-2411-345: The adversary can use a specific word (e.g., ``McDonald") or phrase (e.g., ``teddy bear") as the backdoor trigger.
elements.sent-2411-346: Thus, the backdoor loss for training the backdoor text encoder can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation} \label{eq:backdoor encoder} \mathcal{L}_{E}^{B}=\operatorname{Dist}(E_B(y_t),e_t)\end{equation} where $\operatorname{Dist}$ denotes the distance of two text embeddings\footnote{In this work, we employ Mean Square Error (MSE) to measure this distance.}
elements.sent-2411-347: Hence, we define a normal-functionality loss for training the backdoor text encoder: \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_{E}^{N}=\operatorname{Dist}(E_B(y),E_N(y))\end{equation} where $y$ represents the normal input text without trigger and $E_N$ represents a normal pre-trained text encoder.
elements.sent-2411-348: Therefore, the overall loss function for training the backdoor text encoder can be defined as follows: \begin{equation} \label{eq:overall loss encoder} \mathcal{L}_{E}^{O}=\alpha\mathcal{L}_{E}^{B}+(1-\alpha)\mathcal{L}_{E}^{N}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is used to balance the two loss functions.
elements.sent-2411-349: For the $\operatorname{TriggerText}$, we consider two types of textual backdoor triggers as mentioned in Section \ref{sec:Backdoor Trigger Selection ; For the $\operatorname{TriggerEmb}$, in order to mitigate the impact of the embedded backdoor on the model normal-functionality and enhance stealthiness, we only inject trigger into the first vector of the text embeddings by replacing the first vector of the text embeddings with a vector where each element is 2.}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Backdoor injection process of text encoder} \label{alg:The backdoor injection process of the text encoder} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE $E_N$: the normal pre-trained text encoder; $P_{dataset}$: the poisoned text-image pair dataset; $\alpha$: the hyperparameter for balancing the weights of the loss function; $M$: the epoch of the backdoor training for the backdoor text encoder. \ENSURE the backdoor text encoder $E_B$. \STATE Initialize the backdoor text encoder: $E_B \rightarrow E_N$ \STATE Initialize the training epoch: $i \rightarrow 0$ \WHILE{$i < M$} \FOR{each image-text pair $(x,y) \in P_{dataset}$} \STATE $y_t \rightarrow \operatorname{TriggerText}(y)$ /*Construct the triggered input text.*/ \STATE $e_t \rightarrow \operatorname{TriggerEmb}(E_N(y))$ /*Construct the triggered text embeddings.*/ \STATE Update $E_B$ w.r.t. the overall training loss $\mathcal{L}^{O}_{E}$ \ENDFOR \STATE $i\rightarrow i+1$ \ENDWHILE \RETURN $E_B$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-350: As described in Section \ref{sec:Overview , the objective of the backdoor in the conditional diffusion model is to output backdoor target images for the triggered text embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-351: The backdoor loss for training the backdoor conditional diffusion model can be defined as follows: \begin{equation} \label{eq:Backdoor training loss for diffusion model} \mathcal{L}_{D}^{B}=\mathbb{E}_{z_b, e_t, \epsilon, t}\left[\left\|\epsilon_\theta\left(z_{b,t}, t, e_t\right)-\epsilon\right\|_2^2\right]\end{equation} where $e_t$ represents the triggered text embeddings, $z_{b,t}$ denotes the noisy version of $z_b=\mathcal{E (x_t)$ at the time $t$, $x_t$ denotes the backdoor target image.}
elements.sent-2411-352: For clean text embeddings, the backdoor conditional diffusion model should maintain normal-functionality, i.e., the output latent representation of the backdoor diffusion model should be close to the output of a normal diffusion model: \begin{equation} \label{eq:Normal-functionality training loss for diffusion model} \mathcal{L}_{D}^{N}=\mathbb{E}_{z, E_N, t}\left[\left\|\epsilon_\theta\left(z_t, t, E_N(y)\right)-\epsilon_n\left(z_t, t, E_N(y)\right)\right\|_2^2\right]\end{equation} where $\epsilon_n$ represents a normal pre-trained diffusion model and $E_N$ represents a normal pre-trained text encoder.
elements.sent-2411-353: Hence, the overall loss function for training the backdoor conditional diffusion model can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation} \label{eq:overall loss generator} \mathcal{L}_{D}^{O}=\beta\mathcal{L}_{D}^{B}+(1-\beta)\mathcal{L}_{D}^{N}\end{equation} where $\beta$ is used to balance the two loss functions.
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Backdoor injection process of diffusion model} \label{alg:The backdoor injection process of the diffusion model} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE $E_N$: the normal pre-trained text encoder; $P_{dataset}$: the poisoned text-image pair dataset; $\epsilon_n$: the normal pre-trained diffusion model; $\beta$: the hyperparameter for balancing the weights of the loss function; $N$: the epoch of the backdoor training for the backdoor diffusion model. \ENSURE the backdoor diffusion model $\epsilon_\theta$. \STATE Initialize the backdoor diffusion model: $\epsilon_\theta \rightarrow \epsilon_n$ \STATE Initialize the training epoch: $j \rightarrow 0$ \WHILE{$j < N$} \FOR{each image-text pair $(x,y) \in P_{dataset}$} \STATE $x_t \rightarrow \operatorname{TargetImage}(x)$ /*Set the backdoor target output image.*/ \STATE $e_t \rightarrow \operatorname{TriggerEmb}(E_N(y))$ /*Construct the triggered text embeddings.*/ \STATE Update $\epsilon_\theta$ w.r.t. the overall training loss $\mathcal{L}_{D}^{O}$ \ENDFOR \STATE $j \rightarrow j+1$ \ENDWHILE \RETURN $\epsilon_\theta$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-354: As illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:The workflow of CBACT2I , we consider two types of backdoor attack targets: (1) Specific image.}

elements.sent-2411-356: Backdoor triggering can force the T2I model to generate images of a specific style, e.g., images of Van Gogh style.
elements.sent-2411-357: It is important to note that we also consider more specific and practical backdoor attack targets in the real-world scenario, including bias, harmful, and advertisement contents.
elements.sent-2411-358: These backdoor attack targets are more likely to influence users' views (e.g., for the purpose of commercial advertisement or racist propaganda), thus causing more serious consequences.

elements.sent-2411-391: As shown in Table \ref{Table: Evaluation results under defense ONION , we observe that as the detection threshold $\theta$ increases, the removing rate (RA) also increases to some extent.}
