elements.sent-2411-392: T2Ishieldis \citep{T2IShield is based on a key observation that backdoor trigger token induces an “assimilation phenomenon” in cross-attention maps of the T2I DMs, where the attention of other tokens is suppressed and absorbed by the trigger token.}
elements.sent-2411-393: To quantify the phenomenon, T2IShield introduces two statistical detection methods named FTT and CDA.
elements.sent-2411-394: Specifically, FTT utilizes the Frobenius norm to quantify structural consistency, while CDA employs the covariance matrix to assess structural variations on the Riemannian manifold.
elements.sent-2411-395: UFID \citep{guan2025ufidunifiedframeworkinputlevel detects backdoor samples based on output diversity.}
elements.sent-2411-396: By generating multiple image variations of a sample, UFID constructs a fully connected graph where images serve as nodes and edge weights are images’ similarity.
elements.sent-2411-397: They observe that backdoor samples exhibit higher graph density due to low sensitivity to textual variations.
elements.sent-2411-398: Following the experimental setting of previous works \citep{wang2025dynamic,dai2025practical,zhang2025towards , we chose the most important detection step of T2Ishield for evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-399: For FTT, we set the threshold to 2.5.
elements.sent-2411-400: For CDA, we use a pre-trained linear discriminant analysis model.
elements.sent-2411-401: For UFID, each sample is used to generate 15 variations, and the image similarity is computed using CLIP score.
elements.sent-2411-402: We compute the feature distribution of 3,000 benign samples from DiffusionDB and set the threshold of UFID to 0.776.
elements.sent-2411-403: The diffusion step for a image generation process is set to 50.
elements.sent-2411-404: We evaluate a prompt set containing 3,000 prompts, among which 60 are triggered prompts.
elements.sent-2411-405: For CBACT2I, we consider two types of attack goal: specific style and pre-set image.
elements.sent-2411-406: The SOTA T2I backdoor attack Rickrolling (target prompt attack (TPA) and target attribute attack (TAA)) is also included for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-407: We calculate the F1 score (\%) to evaluate the comprehensive performance of these detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-408: It can be seen from Table \ref{Table: Evaluation results under defense T2Ishield and UFID that T2Ishield and UFID are very effective in detecting Rickrolling (TPA) and CBACT2I (with pre-set image as the backdoor target), but perform poorly in detecting CBACT2I (with specific style as the backdoor target) and Rickrolling (TAA).}
elements.sent-2411-409: As mentioned in previous work \citep{zhai2025efficient , this is because these detection methods rely on the assumption that the backdoor has a stable effect on the entire output image.}
elements.sent-2411-410: When the backdoor target is a specific image or a specific prompt, the entire output image is heavily influenced by the trigger word.
elements.sent-2411-411: For instance, Ricrolling (TPA) replaces the triggered prompt with a backdoor target prompt at the text encoder stage; CBACT2I (with specific image as backdoor target) replaces the entire output image with a specific backdoor target image.
elements.sent-2411-412: Therefore, they are more easily detected by these detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-413: However, when the backdoor target is changing image style, the main content of the image remains unchanged and only the image style has been altered.
elements.sent-2411-414: The influence of the trigger word on the entire output image is relatively small, making these detection methods less effective.

elements.sent-2411-419: CBACT2I is more stealthy and controllable than previous backdoor attacks against T2I models: the backdoor remains dormant in most cases (triggered inputs are also unable to activate the backdoor behavior), it allows the backdoor encoder and decoder to escape detection by defenders.
elements.sent-2411-420: Besides, the adversary can selectively implant the backdoor into specific parts of the T2I customized model, thereby attacking specific model developers.
elements.sent-2411-421: This work reveals the backdoor vulnerabilities of customized T2I models and urges countermeasures to mitigate backdoor threats in this scenario.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2026_conference .}
elements.sent-2411-422: Recently, customization (or personalization) has emerged as a new approach to enable T2I models to learn new concepts and produce images in varied styles.
elements.sent-2411-423: Specifically, regarding the text encoder, recent works often encapsulate new concepts through word embeddings at the input stage of the text encoder \citep{galimage,yang2023controllable ; there are also some researches focus on using different text encoders to recognize input prompts in different languages \citep{carlsson2022cross,yang2022chinese .}}
elements.sent-2411-424: For the DMs, model developers can select different conditional DMs to generate images in various styles \citep{zhang2023adding,sun2023sgdiff .}
elements.sent-2411-425: The reason why the customization approach works is that customized text encoders and customized condition DMs are based on similar fundamental text encoder and condition DM, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-426: The default setting of hyperparameters\footnote{Hyperparameter analysis are presented it in the appendix \ref{sec:Ablation study .}}
elements.sent-2411-427: For backdoor trigger selection, we consider the two types of triggers in our experiments: \textcircled{1 the homoglyphs trigger ``\textcyr{a " (Cyrillic letter alpha, Unicode: U+0430); \textcircled{2 the specific word ``McDonald".}}}
elements.sent-2411-428: For backdoor target images, we consider two kinds of backdoor target images: \textcircled{1 a pre-set specific image, we set a cartoon image of evil (see Figure \ref{Fig:The workflow of CBACT2I ) as the backdoor target image; \textcircled{2 images of a specific style, we set the images of Van Gogh style as the backdoor target images.}}}
elements.sent-2411-429: These two metrics together measure the normal-functionality of the backdoor model, where FID is more weighted towards the quality of the generated images and CLIP-score is more weighted towards the semantic consistency of the generated image and the input text.

elements.sent-2411-430: In CBACT2I, we backdoor fine-tune the text encoder and the diffusion decoder separately.
elements.sent-2411-431: The training of each component is modular and be fine-tuned in parallel, which keeps the training time low.
elements.sent-2411-432: Under the same backdoor fine-tuning settings, we calculated the computational overhead of fine-tuning text encoder alone, fine-tuning diffusion decoder alone, fine-tuning text encoder and diffusion decoder parallelly, and end-to-end training of the entire T2I model, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-433: The training time is shown in Table \ref{Table: Computational overhead (all experiments are implemented in Python and run on a NVIDIA RTX A6000).}
elements.sent-2411-434: The training cost of CBACT2I is comparable to backdoor fine-tuning the diffusion decoder only and much lower than end-to-end backdoor fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-435: Overall, such computational overhead is acceptable for backdoor attackers (lower training times can be achieved with better computing devices).

elements.section-2411-012: Case Study in the Real-world Scenario
elements.sent-2411-444: Furthermore, we measure the attack effectiveness in the real-world scenario by defining the attack success rate and introduce GPT4o to serve as a judge to determine whether the attack is successful or not (this strategy has already been adopted in many works such as MJ-Bench \citep{chen2024mjbench ).}
elements.sent-2411-445: The prompt for judging is as follows: “Whether the doctor in the image is white people”; “Whether the image contains bloody content”; “Whether the car in the image has a Tesla logo”.
elements.sent-2411-446: For each practical attack target, we generate 100 images and feed them to GPT4o for judgement.
elements.sent-2411-447: The attack success rates for considered practical attack targets are presented in Table \ref{Table: Attack success rates for considered practical attack targets .}
elements.sent-2411-448: It can be seen that CBACT2I can achieve good attack performance on these practical attack targets.

elements.sent-2411-449: It should be pointed out that we do not emphasize here that the image steganography (or secret image hiding) scheme based on our combinational backdoor attack has strong advantages over other existing image steganography schemes.
elements.sent-2411-450: The steganography scheme based on our combinational backdoor attack is just a possible positive application, which provides a new research perspective of image steganography.
elements.sent-2411-451: The mechanism behind it is quite different from the existing image steganography schemes, it uses the combinational backdoor model as the secret image carrier.
elements.sent-2411-452: Whether it is better than the existing image steganography schemes needs further exploration.
